document.source_title: Energy bills will rise by £209 in July to £1,850 a year, forecast says
document.summary: Energy bills for households in Great Britain are expected to rise by £209 to £1,850 a year starting in July, driven by increased wholesale energy prices due to the ongoing Iran war. This increase represents a nearly 13% rise from the previous cap of £1,641. Experts warn that even if the conflict ends, prices may not return to previous levels, and households will face higher bills in the autumn as energy usage typically increases.
key_takeaways: Energy bills in Great Britain will rise to £1,850 a year from July.
The increase is attributed to rising wholesale energy prices linked to the Iran war.
The new cap is nearly 13% higher than the previous cap of £1,641.
Experts suggest that even if the Iran war ends, prices may not drop significantly.
There is a call for targeted government support for vulnerable households.
Switching to fixed-rate energy deals may help households save on bills.
document.why_it_matters: This forecast highlights the ongoing impact of geopolitical events on energy prices, emphasizing the need for sustainable energy solutions and government intervention to support vulnerable populations during the cost of living crisis.

claims.claim_text: Energy bills will rise by £209 to £1,850 a year from July.
claims.evidence_text: A typical gas and electricity bill is forecast to reach £1,850 a year from July under the industry regulator Ofgem’s quarterly price cap.

claims.claim_text: The increase is nearly 13% higher than the previous cap of £1,641.
claims.evidence_text: The expected rise is nearly 13% higher than the £1,641 cap on energy bills set for April to June.

claims.claim_text: Even if the Iran war ended, prices may not return to previous levels.
claims.evidence_text: the physical damage to infrastructure, and lingering effect of disrupted supply, means a fall back to April’s price cap levels in the autumn looks unlikely.
